subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with a clear and consistently maintained focus throughout the investigation.
Research question is explicitly stated, narrowly focused, and tightly integrated into each section of the essay.
Methodology is complete and well-considered, using a range of relevant sources and validated instruments (EAT, EDI, DSED).
weaknesses: Minor language lapses and stray symbols momentarily interrupt the flow.
Justification for selecting specific intervention types (e.g., CBT and nutritional counselling) is not fully explained.
Some sources (e.g., Smith and Petrieâs study) are only tangentially relevant to ballet contexts and need stronger contextual justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work consistently meets the top band descriptors for Focus and Method, communicating the topic and research question with clarity and maintaining a coherent methodology. Minor lapses in language and occasional under-justification of source selection prevent a perfect 6, but the overall performance clearly lies at the high end of the 5â6 band.

strengths: Accurate summarisation and referencing of DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
Consistent and correct use of psychological and clinical terminology (e.g., TCA, CBT, OSFED).
Effective use of quantitative data from Ringham et al. and Arcelus et al. to support key claims.
weaknesses: Occasional factual errors (e.g., mis-dating of Rachel et al.) and uncited generalisations about ballet training intensity.
Some reliance on inaccessible full texts limits the depth of source application.
Minor formatting inconsistencies (stray symbols, accent marks) slightly detract from overall precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding shows generally good command of subject matter and terminology, but factual slips, incomplete citations, and occasional over-generalisation prevent consistent precision at the highest band. The work sits solidly in the 3â4 band.

strengths: Analysis synthesises methodological limitations across multiple studies, leading to well-supported comparative conclusions.
Identification of sampling biases (gender, cultural) and evaluation of their implications demonstrates strong critical awareness.
Discussion integrates findings into a reasoned argument advocating multifaceted, culturally adapted interventions.
weaknesses: Key practical considerations (e.g., cost feasibility, attrition bias) are mentioned but not explored in depth.
Absence of a true control group is noted without sufficient discussion of how to address this limitation.
Some evaluative claims (e.g., CBT effectiveness) lack quantitative support from meta-analytic data.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking performance falls within the 7â9 band, with generally strong research relevance, critical analysis, and argumentation. Missing deeper exploration of practical constraints and control-group considerations mean the work does not fully reach the top band, but its coherent, evidence-based evaluation merits a score at the lower end of the 7â9 range.

strengths: Clear overall structure with a comprehensive table of contents and logically labelled sections.
Presence of all major layout elements (title page, word count, reference list) following EE conventions.
Transitions and section headings generally support navigation through the essay.
weaknesses: Inconsistent heading hierarchy and missing page numbers weaken structural precision.
Reference formatting is uneven (broken URLs, missing italics) and stray mathematical notation appears.
Several sub-entries in the contents lack dot leaders and accurate page references.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the basic structural and formatting conventions but contains inconsistencies in heading levels, pagination, and reference styling. This aligns with the lower end of the 3â4 band, warranting a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Psychological and clinical terminologyâTCA, CBT, OSFED, primary/secondary/tertiary prevention, etc.âis used accurately and consistently. Definitions are properly grounded in authoritative sources (e.g., DSM-5), and statistical terms (SD, cohort) are applied correctly.  Minor inconsistencies in accent marks or stray symbols do not substantially detract from clarity.  Overall precision and consistency satisfy the descriptors for the 5-6 band.

Required layout elementsâtitle page, word count, section headings and reference listâare present, and in-text formatting (italics, tables) is generally correct.  However, reference formatting is inconsistent (broken URLs, missing italics), stray mathematical notation appears, and page numbering is absent.  These faults indicate that layout conventions are applied but not flawlessly, justifying a score in the 3-4 band.

The student displays solid subject knowledge, accurately describing DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, CBT principles and prevalence statistics.  Sources are generally applied appropriately to support claims, and contrasting findings (successful vs. limited interventions) are used to build argument.  Nonetheless, occasional factual slips (mis-dating of Rachel et al.), reliance on inaccessible full texts, and some uncited generalisations (e.g., intensity of training) prevent the work from reaching the consistent precision required for the highest band, placing it in the upper middle band.

The methodology shows deliberate, informed selection of sources spanning landmark and recent studies, meta-analyses and clinical manuals (DSM-5).  Inclusion/exclusion criteria (e.g., English-language studies, focus on ballet academies) are explained, and validated instruments (EAT, EDI, DSED) are identified.  Although a few papers are drawn only from abstracts and citation details contain minor errors, the overall range and relevance of sources and the transparent explanation of their use demonstrate a complete, well-considered methodology consistent with band 5-6.

The student presents a clearly-phrased topic, sustained throughout the EE. The introduction contextualises the high prevalence of eating disorders in ballet and links this to the purpose of evaluating psychological interventions, keeping the focus visible in every major section. Minor language lapses (e.g., stray symbols, occasional register shift) momentarily interrupt flow but never obscure meaning. Overall, the topic is communicated accurately and with an appropriate, consistently maintained focus, matching the 5-6 band.

Across nine primary studies and several meta-analyses the student selects research that is, for the most part, directly pertinent to ballet-related eating disorders and intervention efficacy.  Each source is introduced with context and methodological detail, and limitations (sample homogeneity, absence of control groups) are acknowledged.  A few inclusions are only tangentially related or lack full-text access, but the overall selection is purposeful and mostly well-matched to the question, warranting placement in the 7-9 band.

The research question â âÂ¿En quÃ© medida los programas de intervenciÃ³n psicolÃ³gica en academias de ballet han sido efectivos en reducir la incidencia de trastornos alimenticios en sus estudiantes?â â is explicitly stated, narrow in scope and tightly aligned with the ensuing literature review and analysis.  Each section of the paper returns to this question when assessing study outcomes, demonstrating a strong and consistent link between question and discussion.  This clarity and integration sit solidly in the top band for the strand.

The essay contains a full table of contents, clearly labelled sections, and follows EE conventions (abstract omitted per 2016 guide).  Transitions support navigation and the structure mirrors the research process.  Nonetheless, inconsistent heading hierarchy and missing page numbers weaken full compliance.  The work therefore sits at the lower end of the 3-4 band, evidencing an overall appropriate but imperfect structure.

The discussion synthesises findings into a reasoned argument advocating multifaceted, culturally-adapted interventions.  It evaluates methodological constraints, gender bias and generalisability, and suggests concrete directions for future research.  Evaluation is genuinely critical, though some areas (practical feasibility, attrition analysis) could be deeper.  The structure is logical and conclusions stem from the evidence, meeting the descriptors for a strong 7-9 performance.

The analysis compares study designs (longitudinal vs. short-term, CBT vs. nutrition-only) and draws connections to observed outcomes, leading to mostly well-supported conclusions about programme effectiveness.  Strengths and weaknesses (sample size, gender bias, cultural scope) are explicitly dissected.  Some evaluative points remain surface-level (e.g., cost feasibility is mentioned but not explored), preventing the highest depth, but the argument remains coherent and largely evidence-based, aligning with the 7-9 band.

annotations.comment: Absence of a true control group constrains causality claims; recommend including or discussing matched controls to strengthen design.
Math notation appears as HTML rather than LaTeX; format statistical expressions as $$SD = 1.0$$ to meet presentation standards.
The table of contents is comprehensive and follows a logical structure, facilitating navigation through the documentâs sections.
Assertions about the athletic aspects of ballet lack in-text citations; include sources to substantiate claims and strengthen the academic rigour.
DSM-5 diagnostic criteria are accurately summarised and properly referenced to APA (2013), reflecting precise use of subject-specific terminology.
The transition into intervention types lacks justification for why CBT and nutritional counselling were selected; clarify selection criteria in the methodology section.
The objectives of the DancExcellent program are clearly specified, reflecting precise formulation of intervention aims.
Random assignment in Gorrell et al.âs pilot intervention enhances internal validity and supports stronger inferences about intervention effects.
Identification of gender and cultural sampling biases shows strong critical awareness of representational gaps and implications for future studies.
The introduction successfully contextualizes balletâs dual artistic and athletic demands, demonstrating strong subject knowledge and setting a clear research context.
The research question is explicitly and succinctly stated, demonstrating a focused and narrow scope that aligns directly with the investigationâs objectives.
The title is clearly stated at the start of the report, satisfying the requirement for a well-formatted title page and demonstrating adherence to formatting conventions.
A word count is provided and falls within the 4000-word limit, meeting the formatting requirement and reflecting attention to administrative details.
Several sub-entries lack corresponding page numbers and dot leaders, which reduces the precision and completeness of the contents listing.
Effective use of quantitative data from Ringham et al. and Arcelus et al. supports the prevalence discussion and demonstrates proper source application.
The age-range description appears twice with similar wording; consolidate repeated information to improve flow and avoid redundancy.
The sample is 96% white, limiting generalisability; discuss this sampling bias and consider implications for broader populations.
High attrition in Kaufman et al. threatens internal validity; discuss attrition bias and potential differential dropout effects on results.
Citation of the University of Miami source omits the publication year; include the date in parentheses to conform to a consistent referencing style.
The claim that CBT âgenerally is considered effectiveâ would benefit from quantitative evidence (e.g., success rates from meta-analyses) to support this evaluation.
The citation â(Rachel, 2014)â conflicts with later reference to Rachel et al. (2012); verify and correct the publication year to ensure accuracy.
Description of Piranâs multi-level prevention program, including primary, secondary and tertiary components, reflects a comprehensive methodological presentation.
The use of validated instruments (EAT, DSED, EDI) and clear data-collection points underlines a well-considered quasi-experimental methodology.
Relying solely on a study abstract limits depth and reliability; obtain and analyse the full text or clearly note limitations due to incomplete data.
The Torres-McGehee studyâs clear delineation of control and intervention cohorts, with demographic details and pre/post measures, enhances methodological transparency.
The COVID-19 pandemic is noted as a confounder but lacks discussion of mitigation strategies; elaborate on how external events impacted validity.
Smith and Petrieâs study focuses on athletes broadly rather than ballet students; justify its relevance or restrict comparisons to ballet contexts.
Barriers to real-world implementation are identified but not analysed; deepen the evaluation of practical challenges and resource constraints.
The analysis section synthesises methodological limitations across studies, demonstrating a critical and coherent evaluation of research quality.
Recommendations for culturally adapted, personalised programs align directly with research findings and offer actionable implications.
The conclusion effectively summarises key findings, limitations and future research directions, maintaining tight alignment with the research question.
A references section is present, meeting the structural requirement for proper citation of sources.
References show inconsistent italics, broken URLs and missing publication details; apply a uniform citation style (e.g., APA) throughout.
Several entries at the document end are duplicates or incomplete; consolidate redundant lines and verify each citationâs completeness.
Author names and formatting differ from earlier entries; ensure consistency in list order, punctuation and presentation of each reference.
